translation: Whatever is in the heavens and whatever is on the earth is exalting Allah. To Him belongs dominion, and to Him belongs [all] praise, and He is over all things competent
commentary:
يُسَبِّحُ لِلَّهِ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَمَا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ ۖ لَهُ ٱلْمُلْكُ وَلَهُ ٱلْحَمْدُ ۖ وَهُوَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرٌ

This Surah represents some of the most beautiful fruits of genuine Iman. When somebody truly tastes faith, what are the joys they experience in life? This Surah describes them powerfully. The previous Surah was about the bankruptcy of Iman - there was no faith left, only hypocrisy. This Surah is the opposite. It's about Iman itself.

Everything in the skies and the earth continues to declare the perfection of Allah ﷻ. The verb يُسَبِّحُ  is in the present continuous tense, meaning this glorification never stops. To Him alone belongs all dominion, and to Him alone belongs all praise and gratitude. وَهُوَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرٌ - and He is in complete control over all things.

translation: It is He who created you, and among you is the disbeliever, and among you is the believer. And Allah, of what you do, is Seeing
commentary:
هُوَ ٱلَّذِى خَلَقَكُمْ فَمِنكُمْ كَافِرٌ وَمِنكُم مُّؤْمِنٌ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ بَصِيرٌ

He is the One who created you. فَمِنكُمْ كَافِرٌ وَمِنكُم مُّؤْمِنٌ - then out of you there is someone who disbelieves, and out of you there is someone who believes. In other words, believers and disbelievers - it doesn't matter who. Either way, He created all of you.

The profound question embedded here is: How can you disbelieve even though He created all of you? Some come out disbelievers and some come out believers. Notice that the majority are disbelievers, as كَافِرٌ (disbeliever) is mentioned first. This connects back to the style of the Musabbihaat - those surahs that begin with declaring Allah's perfection and how everything else is doing what it's supposed to do. But look at you people! Allah ﷻ created the skies and the earth, and they continue to declare His perfection. But among you, so many disbelieve while some believe.

وَٱللَّهُ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ بَصِيرٌ - Allah ﷻ is in full view of what you're up to. He sees everything you do.

translation: He created the heavens and earth in truth and formed you and perfected your forms; and to Him is the [final] destination
commentary:
خَلَقَ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضَ بِٱلْحَقِّ وَصَوَّرَكُمْ فَأَحْسَنَ صُوَرَكُمْ ۖ وَإِلَيْهِ ٱلْمَصِيرُ

He created the skies and the earth بِٱلْحَقِّ - with purpose, with truth. وَصَوَّرَكُمْ - and He molded you, He fashioned you. فَأَحْسَنَ صُوَرَكُمْ - and He perfected how He molded you.

This molding is comprehensive. Some of you were made short, some tall. The size of your nose, the color of your eyes, the shape of your hands - all of this is the molding that Allah ﷻ did of you, and how beautifully He molded us. Some scholars even extend this understanding of صُوَرَكُمْ to include not just physical form but personality as well. Some He made artistically inclined, some mathematically inclined. Some humorous, some serious. He did beautiful molding of all your personalities and the diversity you possess.

وَإِلَيْهِ ٱلْمَصِيرُ - and to Him alone is the final return.

The concept of أَحْسَنَ صُوَرَكُمْ should make us deeply grateful for whatever physical shape we have. "I wish I was lighter skinned, I wish I was darker skinned, I wish I was taller, shorter, this or that" - these complaints miss the point entirely. Allah ﷻ fashioned you this way. Now, if you don't take care of yourself, that's something else. You can't say "Allah fashioned me this way" about things you had control over. Allah ﷻ fashioned you in a particular way, but you could have brushed your teeth, for example. That's on you. But the fundamental design - that's from Allah ﷻ, and it deserves gratitude.

translation: He knows what is within the heavens and earth and knows what you conceal and what you declare. And Allah is Knowing of that within the breasts
commentary:
يَعْلَمُ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ وَيَعْلَمُ مَا تُسِرُّونَ وَمَا تُعْلِنُونَ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ عَلِيمٌۢ بِذَاتِ ٱلصُّدُورِ

He knows what's in the skies and the earth. وَيَعْلَمُ مَا تُسِرُّونَ وَمَا تُعْلِنُونَ - and He knows the things you hide and the things you make public. The secrets you keep and the things you announce openly. وَٱللَّهُ عَلِيمٌۢ بِذَاتِ ٱلصُّدُورِ - and Allah ﷻ is fully knowledgeable of the very nature of the chest.

Look at the progression Allah ﷻ presents about His knowledge of us. First, He created us. Then He molded us. When He molded us, it implies He knew us intimately. He is more knowledgeable of the workings of my body than I am myself. He was molding me and designing me in the belly of my mother while I had nothing to do with it, and my mother had nothing to do with it. A child is born and people come over and say, "Oh, it's so cute!" and the parent says "Thank you, thank you" - like they had something to do with it! It doesn't work like that. You were molded by Allah ﷻ as a design, and how beautifully you were molded.

But now that you've been made, your conscious and subconscious personality, your inner motives, your deepest, darkest secrets - مَا تُسِرُّونَ وَمَا تُعْلِنُونَ - He knows the very nature of the chest. We know something about ourselves, yet we don't know so much about ourselves. We see dreams and don't even know what they mean. We have thoughts and don't know where they came from. People say "A creative idea just came to me" - a multimillion dollar idea just appeared in my head. Where did it come from? Allah ﷻ knows the nature of the chest. He brings about creativity.

One of the great manifestations and signs of Allah's power is not just nature itself, but human creations - architecture, art, the development of cities, technologies. These are also a marvel of Allah's creation because Allah's creation made those creations. Who fired those neurons? Who gave those thoughts? Even your iPhone can increase your Iman because it's a creation of the human mind that Allah ﷻ brought here. A thousand years ago, it was just dirt and metal. That's all it was. And we use this dirt and metal, by Allah's inspiration of the human being, to do amazing things.

Planes should increase your Iman. You see how heavy a plane is? And it's flying! It's remarkable. If you talked to people four or five hundred years ago, a thousand years ago, about human beings - an entire caravan of people with all their luggage - flying across the ocean, they would think it was a fairy tale. SubhanAllah, what Allah ﷻ has inspired the human being to do.

translation: Has there not come to you the news of those who disbelieved before? So they tasted the bad consequence of their affair, and they will have a painful punishment
commentary:
أَلَمْ يَأْتِكُمْ نَبَؤُا۟ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ مِن قَبْلُ فَذَاقُوا۟ وَبَالَ أَمْرِهِمْ وَلَهُمْ عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ

Didn't the news of those who came long before you and disbelieved reach you? فَذَاقُوا۟ وَبَالَ أَمْرِهِمْ - Then they tasted the catastrophe of their decision. وَلَهُمْ عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ - and they will have painful torment, painful punishment.

translation: That is because their messengers used to come to them with clear evidences, but they said, "Shall human beings guide us?" and disbelieved and turned away. And Allah dispensed [with them]; and Allah is Free of need and Praiseworthy
commentary:
ذَٰلِكَ بِأَنَّهُۥ كَانَت تَّأْتِيهِمْ رُسُلُهُم بِٱلْبَيِّنَـٰتِ فَقَالُوٓا۟ أَبَشَرٌ يَهْدُونَنَا فَكَفَرُوا۟ وَتَوَلَّوا۟ ۚ وَّٱسْتَغْنَى ٱللَّهُ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ غَنِىٌّ حَمِيدٌ

ذَٰلِكَ بِأَنَّهُۥ كَانَت تَّأْتِيهِمْ رُسُلُهُم بِٱلْبَيِّنَـٰتِ - That is because messengers used to come to them with the clearest proof. فَقَالُوٓا۟ أَبَشَرٌ يَهْدُونَنَا - And they would say, "بَشَرٌ is going to guide us?" Another human being is going to guide us?

The word بَشَرٌ comes from the Arabic word بِشْر , which means skin. The human being is called bashar because the skin shows more than in other creatures. The gorilla's skin is covered in fur. The bull, the lion, other animals - they're covered in fur. Our skin shows more. We're not covered in fur as much, so we're called bashar. This is the Arabic expression for mortal being - flesh and bone, as we say in English.

So people would say, "Flesh and bone is going to guide me? They're going to guide us?" This reveals a deep reality and one of the real causes for people not accepting the Deen. Because religion did not come from Allah ﷻ directly to me - religion came from Allah ﷻ to messengers, and from messengers to me. There's an intermediary in delivering the Deen, and that is the messenger.

The thing is, the messenger is a human being. And human beings have this inherent problem with accepting the authority of other human beings. We want to be free. We like to be independent. We just don't like authority. As we grow older, we have a hard time listening to our parents. "Come here." "No, I want to be free!" People hate cops. They see a cop and they get angry. "This cop is making me slow down, indicate when I change lanes, stop at a stop sign." Why the resentment? Because he has authority. People don't like teachers giving homework because they're exercising authority. "Get back in class!" "Oh, come on!" People don't like governments. Why not? Authority.

People don't like their bosses, more often than not. They don't like their managers because they tell them what to do. Inside the household, sometimes the wife can't stand her husband - usually loves him, loves him all the time, except when he tells her something to do. She gets so mad. "Why are you telling me what to do? You're not the boss of me!"

If that's the case - and all of those instances of authority are limited - a husband's authority is limited, a father's authority is limited, a government's authority is limited. But a Prophet's authority among human beings - if there is an entity that has more authority over believers than any other, it has to be a Prophet. Because he doesn't just tell you what to do and what not to do. He dictates what you can love, what you can hate, how you should wake up, how you should go to sleep, what you should learn, what you shouldn't learn, what you should say, what you shouldn't say.

In any other relationship, the scope of instructions is limited. My boss tells me what I should work on and what I shouldn't work on, and it's over. Even in a master-slave relationship, the master usually has a limited set of instructions: work on the farm, go live over there, do this. And you have some free time - do whatever you want.

A messenger dictates every aspect of life through revelation. He demands, in some sense, an absolute form of obedience. We read سَمِعْنَا وَأَطَعْنَا - "We hear and we obey" - but we think we heard Allah ﷻ and we obeyed. But we didn't actually hear Allah ﷻ directly. Who did we hear? The messenger, practically speaking. You have to hear that man.

This is one of the greatest hurdles that the Companions (RA) crossed with flying colors - though I don't know how we would have done. The Prophet ﷺ was a member of a community, a member of a family. He was a father. He was a husband. He was a friend. He was a nephew. He was a cousin. He had all of these relationships that you and I have. But once he became a messenger, his wife couldn't think of him as just a husband first - she had to think of him as the Messenger first. Then she could think of him as a husband.

It's not like she stopped thinking of him as a husband - she did. They had arguments too. Marital discord was there. It happened. There are some remarkable stories about Aisha (RA) in our tradition. They got into an argument once, and he said, "We should get somebody to help us counsel. We should get a counselor." The Prophet ﷺ - the counselor to humanity - says to his wife, "We should get a counselor," which means nobody is above counseling.

So she says, "Okay, what do you think? Who should we get?" He said, "We should get Abu Ubayda." She says, "No, he loves you too much. We're not getting Abu Ubayda." So he says, "Okay, you pick." So she says, "My dad, Abu Bakr (RA)."

So what do they call? Work-at-home dad's going to counsel. The Prophet ﷺ says, "Why don't you start, Aisha?" She says, "No, I want you to start so I can make sure you get it right."

Abu Bakr (RA), when he heard that, was about to grab and strangle her. "Nobody talks to my messenger this way!" The Prophet ﷺ grabs Abu Bakr and says, "Leave! We don't need your help anymore. Please just go, just go!" And that solved their problem.

The point is, they didn't think of each other as spouse before they thought of each other as messenger and believer. She never thought of him as just a husband. You never find a Hadith where Aisha (RA) says "قَالَ زَوْجِي" - "My husband said." No. "قَالَ رَسُولُ ٱللَّٰهِ" - "The Messenger of Allah said." His uncle never says, "My nephew said." He says, "The Messenger of Allah said." The best friend from childhood never says, "My buddy said, my close friend said." No. "The Messenger of Allah said."

They had to think of the Prophet ﷺ differently from how they were used to thinking of him for forty years. They had to transform their thought. But disbelievers could not think of him differently. "He's just a guy. What do you mean all of a sudden he is the absolute authority speaking on behalf of God? What do you mean? That doesn't make any sense to me."

So they would say, "أَبَشَرٌ يَهْدُونَنَا - He's going to guide us? He's just like me!" What did they say about Isa (AS)? "He walks around, he goes shopping. He's a messenger? Look at him! I saw him at the mall. He was in the cafeteria eating food. Messenger of God? Really? He's just like you and me." That was their argument.

One of the greatest teachings of our religion is that humility is not just to Allah ﷻ - humility is to the messenger ﷺ. Humility is to the authority of a human being, the Messenger ﷺ. And without that, you cannot really have Iman. You can't.

The hypocrites, by the way, interestingly enough, wanted to make a distinction between Allah ﷻ and His messenger ﷺ. When the Prophet ﷺ would give instructions, they would ask, "Is this Allah or is that you?" Nowadays people say, "Is that in the Quran or Hadith?" Let me translate that in English for you: "Should I take it seriously or not?" That's the translation of "Is that in the Quran or is that Hadith?" In the time of the Prophet ﷺ, the hypocrites would ask him to his face, "Is this Quran or is this you? So we know whether it's just you."

This hypocrisy already existed - a dismissal of the validity and importance of the Sunnah. You can talk about the authenticity of a particular narration. You can talk about the proper context of it. That's something else. But dismissing the Sunnah itself is an act of hypocrisy. "It's just him talking. We don't have to follow him."

No - أَبَشَرٌ يَهْدُونَنَا - "This guy is going to guide us?" فَكَفَرُوا۟ - So they denied. وَتَوَلَّوا۟ - And they turned away. وَّٱسْتَغْنَى ٱللَّهُ - And Allah ﷻ declared that He's free of need. وَٱللَّهُ غَنِىٌّ حَمِيدٌ - Allah ﷻ is free of need, praised in and of Himself.

translation: Those who disbelieve have claimed that they will never be resurrected. Say, "Yes, by my Lord, you will surely be resurrected; then you will surely be informed of what you did. And that, for Allah, is easy
commentary:
زَعَمَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوٓا۟ أَن لَّن يُبْعَثُوا۟ ۚ قُلْ بَلَىٰ وَرَبِّى لَتُبْعَثُنَّ ثُمَّ لَتُنَبَّؤُنَّ بِمَا عَمِلْتُمْ ۚ وَذَٰلِكَ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ يَسِيرٌ

The first problem was pride against the messenger. The second problem, of course, is the belief that there's no consequence for my behavior.

زَعَمَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوٓا۟ أَن لَّن يُبْعَثُوا۟ - These disbelievers have confidently assumed that they're not going to be raised. قُلْ بَلَىٰ - Tell them: No! On the contrary! Not at all! بَلَىٰ means on the contrary, absolutely not.

وَرَبِّى لَتُبْعَثُنَّ - I swear by my Master, you will absolutely be raised! Allah ﷻ usually swears by His creation, but in these words, the Messenger ﷺ is commanded to say, "I swear by my Rabb." وَرَبِّى - by my Master.

Something important about oaths: the object of the oath is proof of the subject. The object of the oath here is Allah ﷻ Himself - رَبِّى, my Master. My Master Himself, on account of the fact that He is Master, is enough proof that He will raise. If we have a Master, it means He has authority. And if He has authority, then I will be answerable for violating that authority.

So if Allah ﷻ really is the Master and I do whatever I want on this earth and then die - if He's a real Master, then as Master, He will pull me back out of this earth and ask me what I did. Believing in Allah ﷻ as Master necessitates believing in the Afterlife. Allah ﷻ uses the word رَبّ  here as evidence of being raised.

لَتُبْعَثُنَّ ثُمَّ لَتُنَبَّؤُنَّ بِمَا عَمِلْتُمْ - You will absolutely be raised, then you will thoroughly be informed of all the things you used to do. وَذَٰلِكَ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ يَسِيرٌ - And that is really easy for Allah ﷻ. For Allah ﷻ, that is extremely easy.

translation: So believe in Allah and His Messenger and the Qur'an which We have sent down. And Allah is Acquainted with what you do
commentary:
فَـَٔامِنُوا۟ بِٱللَّهِ وَرَسُولِهِۦ وَٱلنُّورِ ٱلَّذِىٓ أَنزَلْنَا ۚ وَٱللَّهُ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ خَبِيرٌ

فَـَٔامِنُوا۟ بِٱللَّهِ وَرَسُولِهِۦ - Then believe in Allah ﷻ and His Messenger.

This is actually remarkable. It sounds like it's talking to disbelievers, right? Earlier in the Surah we read: فَمِنكُمْ كَافِرٌ وَمِنكُم مُّؤْمِنٌ - some of you are disbelievers, some of you are believers. It doesn't matter. The command here is like Allah ﷻ taking us back to the basics of Iman: Believe in Allah ﷻ and His Messenger!

وَٱلنُّورِ ٱلَّذِىٓ أَنزَلْنَا - And the light that We have sent down. The Quran is called light in this ayah - النُّور, the light that We sent down.

وَٱللَّهُ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ خَبِيرٌ - And Allah ﷻ is fully aware of what you do.

translation: The Day He will assemble you for the Day of Assembly - that is the Day of Deprivation. And whoever believes in Allah and does righteousness - He will remove from him his misdeeds and admit him to gardens beneath which rivers flow, wherein they will abide forever. That is the great attainment
commentary:
يَوْمَ يَجْمَعُكُمْ لِيَوْمِ ٱلْجَمْعِ ۖ ذَٰلِكَ يَوْمُ ٱلتَّغَابُنِ ۗ وَمَن يُؤْمِنۢ بِٱللَّهِ وَيَعْمَلْ صَـٰلِحًا يُكَفِّرْ عَنْهُ سَيِّـَٔاتِهِۦ وَيُدْخِلْهُ جَنَّـٰتٍ تَجْرِى مِن تَحْتِهَا ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرُ خَـٰلِدِينَ فِيهَآ أَبَدًا ۚ ذَٰلِكَ ٱلْفَوْزُ ٱلْعَظِيمُ

The day of gathering is coming—يَوْمَ يَجْمَعُكُمْ لِيَوْمِ ٱلْجَمْعِ (the Day when He will gather you for the Day of Gathering). There's something striking about this repetition. Allah ﷻ gathers us for a day that is specifically designated for gathering. This isn't just any assembly; this is THE gathering, unlike any that has ever occurred or will ever occur. The emphasis is on the magnitude and finality of this event.

We're supposed to rush to Friday prayer voluntarily, making that weekly gathering a priority in our lives. But there's a day coming when the gathering won't be voluntary at all—you'll show up whether you want to or not. That's يَوْمُ ٱلْجَمْعِ, the ultimate Day of Assembly.

Then Allah ﷻ gives this day its proper name: ذَٰلِكَ يَوْمُ ٱلتَّغَابُنِ (That is the Day of Mutual Loss and Gain). This is the Day of Ultimate Loss and Ultimate Victory. It's when the real results come out, when the final grades are revealed. Nobody will have ever won like that day's winner, and nobody will have ever lost like that day's loser. These are two extremes with nothing in between.

The ayah continues with a promise: وَمَن يُؤْمِنۢ بِٱللَّهِ وَيَعْمَلْ صَـٰلِحًا (And whoever believes in Allah and does righteous deeds). Notice how the conditions are stated—both iman and righteous action together. Then comes the reward: يُكَفِّرْ عَنْهُ سَيِّـَٔاتِهِۦ (He will remove from him his sins). Allah ﷻ will bury away, completely wipe out the sins of this person.

وَيُدْخِلْهُ جَنَّـٰتٍ تَجْرِى مِن تَحْتِهَا ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرُ (And He will admit him into gardens beneath which rivers flow). Everything up to this point has been in the singular—he will remove HIS sins, He will enter HIM into gardens. It's all individual, personal, one-on-one with Allah ﷻ.

But then suddenly we get خَـٰلِدِينَ فِيهَآ أَبَدًا (They will abide therein forever)—plural! Why the switch from singular to plural? Because you're judged individually, you enter Jannah based on your own account, your own deeds, your own faith. But once you're inside, the experience becomes collective. One of the great joys of Jannah is the community, meeting others, hearing their stories. Imagine meeting the Companions (RA) and asking them to walk you through their experiences. The gathering in Jannah is part of its beauty, part of its reward.

ذَٰلِكَ ٱلْفَوْزُ ٱلْعَظِيمُ (That is the great achievement). This is the ultimate success, the goal of all goals.

translation: But the ones who disbelieved and denied Our verses - those are the companions of the Fire, abiding eternally therein; and wretched is the destination
commentary:
وَٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ وَكَذَّبُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَآ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلنَّارِ خَـٰلِدِينَ فِيهَا ۖ وَبِئْسَ ٱلْمَصِيرُ

The contrast is stark and immediate. وَٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ وَكَذَّبُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَآ (And those who disbelieved and denied Our signs)—notice both verbs are used. It's not just passive disbelief; it's active rejection and denial of Allah's revelations and signs.

أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلنَّارِ (Those are the companions of the Fire). Just as believers become companions of Paradise, the disbelievers become companions of the Fire. خَـٰلِدِينَ فِيهَا (They will abide therein eternally). The same word for eternal residence, but what a difference in destination.

وَبِئْسَ ٱلْمَصِيرُ (And wretched is the destination). What a horrible place to end up, what a terrible final abode. These are now the fruits of iman versus kufr—the ultimate outcomes, the final results of the choices we make in this life.

translation: No disaster strikes except by permission of Allah. And whoever believes in Allah - He will guide his heart. And Allah is Knowing of all things
commentary:
مَآ أَصَابَ مِن مُّصِيبَةٍ إِلَّا بِإِذْنِ ٱللَّهِ ۗ وَمَن يُؤْمِنۢ بِٱللَّهِ يَهْدِ قَلْبَهُۥ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ بِكُلِّ شَىْءٍ عَلِيمٌ

This ayah addresses one of the most challenging aspects of faith: dealing with calamity. مَآ أَصَابَ مِن مُّصِيبَةٍ إِلَّا بِإِذْنِ ٱللَّهِ (Not a single calamity strikes except by Allah's permission). The word أَصَابَ is significant—it comes from the root meaning to hit a target with an arrow. When an archer aims and hits exactly what he intended to hit, that's إصابة. So Allah ﷻ is telling us that calamities are targeted, they're precise, they're meant for specific people at specific times.

This means there's no such thing as random suffering. There's no "if only I had left five minutes earlier" or "if only I had gone left instead of right." All those "what ifs" that plague us after something difficult happens—they're meaningless because what happened was meant to happen. It was targeted at you specifically. إِلَّا بِإِذْنِ ٱللَّهِ—except by Allah's permission, by His decree.

Now here's the crucial part: وَمَن يُؤْمِنۢ بِٱللَّهِ يَهْدِ قَلْبَهُۥ (And whoever believes in Allah, He will guide his heart). Notice what Allah ﷻ is telling us—when calamity strikes, that's when most people lose their faith. That's when the "why me?" questions start. "Why would Allah ﷻ do this to me?" "Why does He allow suffering?" The problem of human suffering is the number one argument used by people who reject faith, both in philosophy and in personal life. Philosophers ask: "If God is so merciful, how can there be cancer, natural disasters, innocent suffering?" And individuals ask: "Where was Allah ﷻ when I needed Him? How could He let this happen to me?"

But Allah ﷻ is saying something extraordinary here: if you can maintain your faith THROUGH calamity, if you can hold on to your belief in Allah ﷻ when things are falling apart, then He will guide your heart. يَهْدِ قَلْبَهُۥ—He will guide specifically the heart, that most fluctuating, unstable part of the human being. The heart changes moment to moment, affected by everything around us. But Allah ﷻ promises to stabilize it, to guide it, if we can maintain iman during hardship.

Guidance of the heart is one of the greatest treasures mentioned in the Quran. It's one of the most valuable gifts Allah ﷻ can give. And He's guaranteeing it to anyone who can survive calamity with their faith intact.

The ayah ends with وَٱللَّهُ بِكُلِّ شَىْءٍ عَلِيمٌ (And Allah is Knowing of all things). This isn't a cold statement. It's a profound comfort. Allah ﷻ isn't oblivious to your pain. He's not unaware of what you're going through. He knows everything—every tear, every struggle, every moment of suffering. He is fully knowledgeable of all things.

Consider the story of Yusuf (AS) as a living commentary on this ayah. His entire life was one calamity after another—kidnapped as a child, sold as a slave, falsely accused despite his innocence, imprisoned for years despite being proven innocent. At every single point, you have the right to ask: what did he do to deserve this? What was his crime? His father Yaqub (AS) suffered the pain of losing his beloved son for decades. Where's the justice in that? How could Allah ﷻ allow such a righteous father to suffer so much?

Some scholars say the entire Surah Yusuf is the tafsir of this one ayah. The name Yusuf itself may come from أسف, meaning overwhelmed by sorrow, because his life contained so many sad occasions. Imagine spending years in a dark dungeon—we read those ayahs quickly, but those years weren't quick for him. فَلَبِثَ فِي السِّجْنِ بِضْعَ سِنِينَ (So he remained in prison several years)—that "while" was a long while.

But here's the key: Yusuf (AS) maintained his faith. He never lost his iman through all that suffering. Neither did his father. And because they maintained their faith, Allah ﷻ guided their hearts and brought them to an ending more beautiful than they could have imagined.

translation: And obey Allah and obey the Messenger; but if you turn away - then upon Our Messenger is only [the duty of] clear notification
commentary:
وَأَطِيعُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَأَطِيعُوا۟ ٱلرَّسُولَ ۚ فَإِن تَوَلَّيْتُمْ فَإِنَّمَا عَلَىٰ رَسُولِنَا ٱلْبَلَـٰغُ ٱلْمُبِينُ

Right after discussing calamity and maintaining faith, Allah ﷻ tells us HOW to maintain that faith: وَأَطِيعُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَأَطِيعُوا۟ ٱلرَّسُولَ (Obey Allah and obey the Messenger). This is the practical application. When calamity strikes, when you're going through difficulty, that's when you need to increase your obedience even more. That's when you should think: "How have I been disobeying Allah ﷻ? Maybe He's giving me an opportunity to get my act together, to return to Him, to guarantee that guidance of the heart by obeying Him again."

We oversimplify the struggles of the Companions (RA) so much. Yes, they gave their lives on the battlefield and went straight to Jannah—but did they not leave families behind? When a Companion (RA) was martyred, didn't his wife receive the news that her husband was gone forever? Didn't his mother get word that her son was no longer there? As happy as they were that their loved one was headed to Jannah, wasn't there also sadness that they wouldn't see them again in this life? That's real calamity, real hardship, even for the most faithful.

And here's what's remarkable: it's not just calamity that comes from sinning. Yes, sometimes people sin and face difficulties, and they correct themselves. But the Companions (RA) were doing wonderful things, amazing acts of worship and sacrifice, and they still faced calamities. So you can't say, "I'm a good person, why is Allah ﷻ giving me a hard time? I don't do anything bad, so why does He punish me like this?"

It's not a punishment. Among the people there are those who, when they face pain or hardship, assume that the suffering is divine punishment, that Allah ﷻ is angry with them. That's not necessarily the case. These tests come to those who do good and those who do bad. But for the believers, the opportunity is being offered: this is your chance to guarantee guidance of the heart.

فَإِن تَوَلَّيْتُمْ فَإِنَّمَا عَلَىٰ رَسُولِنَا ٱلْبَلَـٰغُ ٱلْمُبِينُ (And if you turn away, then upon Our Messenger is only clear communication). The Prophet ﷺ has done his job. He's delivered the message clearly. The choice is now ours.

translation: Allah - there is no deity except Him. And upon Allah let the believers rely
commentary:
ٱللَّهُ لَآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ ۚ وَعَلَى ٱللَّهِ فَلْيَتَوَكَّلِ ٱلْمُؤْمِنُونَ

Allah ﷻ brings us back to the foundation: ٱللَّهُ لَآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ (Allah—there is no deity except Him). There is no one worthy of worship, no one to be obeyed unconditionally, no one to place ultimate trust in except Allah ﷻ alone.

And then the logical conclusion: وَعَلَى ٱللَّهِ فَلْيَتَوَكَّلِ ٱلْمُؤْمِنُونَ (And upon Allah let the believers rely). If you truly believe in لَآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ, how can you not have complete reliance on Allah ﷻ? How can believers not place their full trust and confidence in their Lord's plan?

This connects directly to the calamity discussion. When difficulty strikes, we have to remember that we have plans, but Allah ﷻ has His own plans—and His plans are always better than ours, even when we can't see it. You might plan out your entire career path: go to this school, get these interviews, follow these steps, and you'll end up exactly where you want to be. But then Allah ﷻ has a different plan. You get fired the first week. You can't find another job. You have to move to another state. Things happen that you never expected, and eventually you end up doing something completely different—and you're happier than you ever imagined you could be.

The mother of Musa (AS) had a plan to save her child: hide him, keep him safe from Firaun's soldiers. But Allah ﷻ had a completely different plan: throw him in the river. Who would agree with that plan? What mother would think that's a good idea? Throw a helpless baby in the river? But Allah ﷻ put certainty in her heart—don't worry, he'll come back to you. If she had told her neighbors, "I had a feeling Allah ﷻ wants me to throw my baby in the river, but don't worry, Allah ﷻ guaranteed he's coming back to me," they would have thought she'd lost her mind.

But hours later, she was reunited with her son. Hours later, she was holding him again, and she knew for herself that Allah's promise was true. She had her plan to save her child, but Allah ﷻ had a much better plan—one she couldn't foresee, one that didn't make sense to her in the moment, but one that worked perfectly.

And notice: Allah ﷻ told her He would return her son and make him one of the messengers, but He didn't tell her WHERE the baby would be delivered. He didn't say, "He's going to Firaun's palace." If He had included that detail—"Don't worry, I'll return him to you, but first he's going to Firaun's castle"—the fear and grief would never have left her. Allah ﷻ kept her on a need-to-know basis because He knew what her heart could handle.

That's tawakkul. That's what it means to rely on Allah ﷻ. The Companions (RA) didn't see an end in sight to their problems. They didn't know that things would work out. The Battle of Badr could have been the end. The Battle of Uhud could have been the end. The Treaty of Hudaybiyyah could have been the end. The Hijrah itself could have been the end—there were so many moments where one move here or there and the entire mission could have failed. There were so many vulnerabilities where everything could have fallen apart.

But the only thing moving it forward was tawakkul in Allah ﷻ. That's it. Complete reliance on Him. And that's the real fruit of iman—learning to rely on Allah ﷻ in all circumstances, whether things are going well or falling apart.

translation: O you who have believed, indeed, among your wives and your children are enemies to you, so beware of them. But if you pardon and overlook and forgive - then indeed, Allah is Forgiving and Merciful
commentary:
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟ إِنَّ مِنْ أَزْوَٰجِكُمْ وَأَوْلَـٰدِكُمْ عَدُوًّا لَّكُمْ فَٱحْذَرُوهُمْ ۚ وَإِن تَعْفُوا۟ وَتَصْفَحُوا۟ وَتَغْفِرُوا۟ فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ

*O you who believe, indeed among your spouses and your children are enemies to you, so beware of them. But if you pardon and overlook and forgive, then indeed Allah is Forgiving and Merciful.*

This ayah addresses a reality that catches many believers off guard: some of those closest to us can become obstacles in our relationship with Allah ﷻ. The word أَزْوَاج  refers to spouses, both husbands and wives. This is not a gender-specific warning. The Quran itself mentions examples from both sides: the wives of Lut (AS) and Nuh (AS) were obstacles to their husbands' missions, while on the other hand, we have examples of righteous women married to difficult men.

What does it mean for a spouse or child to be an enemy? This is not referring to someone who simply has different preferences or habits. The enmity here is spiritual in nature. It refers to when family members pull you away from Allah ﷻ through materialistic demands and worldly distractions. When a spouse pressures you to abandon religious commitments for financial gain, or manipulates religious knowledge to serve materialistic ends, they become an obstacle between you and your Creator.

However, knowledge alone does not protect against this. A person can be highly educated about Islam yet use that knowledge as a tool for manipulation rather than spiritual growth. The true measure is not how much someone knows, but how they apply that knowledge in building a God-conscious household.

The remedy Allah ﷻ prescribes is profound in its simplicity, yet challenging in its application. He provides three progressive steps: تَعْفُوا۟ , تَصْفَحُوا۟ , and تَغْفِرُوا۟.

The first, ta'fu, means to lovingly pardon. This is about choosing not to bring up every minor irritation. Modern culture encourages expressing every feeling, but this ayah teaches us something different: wisdom in what we choose to voice. Not every mistake needs to be mentioned. If your spouse forgets something minor, simply take care of it yourself without making it an issue. The strength to overlook small matters is a sign of emotional maturity and love.

The second step, tasfahoo, comes from the word صفح , meaning a page. To turn the page means to truly let go of what has already been addressed. Once an issue is resolved, it must be buried completely. Do not keep a mental record of past mistakes to bring up during future disagreements. This is the toxic pattern that destroys marriages: the same argument recycled every few months because neither person has truly turned the page. When Allah ﷻ commands us to turn the page, He means the previous page should no longer be visible.

The third level, taghfiru, literally means to cover. This word comes from the same root as the helmet that protects a warrior's head. Just as a helmet covers and protects, we are called to cover the mistakes of our spouses. When they err, especially in matters of religion, protect their dignity. Don't expose their faults or keep score of their shortcomings.

These three steps are difficult. They require us to see beyond our own hurt feelings and ego. But notice what follows: فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ (indeed Allah is Forgiving and Merciful). If you want Allah's forgiveness and mercy, you must embody these qualities in your marriage. You cannot expect from Allah ﷻ what you refuse to give to your spouse.

When emotional health is absent from a marriage, it poisons everything else. Your career suffers. Your service to the community becomes burdensome. Your friendships feel hollow. Even food loses its taste and sunny days appear gloomy. Everything in life becomes colored by the misery at home. This is why these three principles are not optional luxuries but essential survival tools for a marriage.

The deeper wisdom here is about accountability. We often think: "I am answerable to my spouse for how I treat them, and they are answerable to me." But the reality is different. Your behavior toward your spouse is being graded by Allah ﷻ, and their behavior toward you is being graded by Allah ﷻ. You should not wish for your spouse to receive a bad grade from Allah ﷻ. Even when they hurt you or disobey you, your heart should turn to Allah ﷻ with a plea: "O Allah, forgive them. I don't want to see them suffer on the Day of Judgment. I love them too much for that."

This same principle extends to children. When your children disobey, your instinct might be to feel vindicated, to want Allah ﷻ to see their disobedience. But no parent should wish punishment upon their child. The correct response is to ask Allah ﷻ for the strength to forgive them, and to seek His forgiveness for them as well.

This is real love: when you are not obsessed with yourself, when even your hurt feelings take second place to your concern for the other person's standing before Allah ﷻ.

translation: Your wealth and your children are but a trial, and Allah has with Him a great reward
commentary:
إِنَّمَآ أَمْوَٰلُكُمْ وَأَوْلَـٰدُكُمْ فِتْنَةٌ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ عِندَهُۥٓ أَجْرٌ عَظِيمٌ

*Your wealth and your children are but a trial, and Allah has with Him a great reward.*

After addressing the potential for spouses to become spiritual obstacles, Allah ﷻ now broadens the scope to include wealth and children. The word فِتْنَة  is often understood as something evil, and indeed the Quran uses it in that sense elsewhere. But the root meaning of fitnah is actually a test or trial, specifically the process of purifying gold. When gold is purified, it must be heated, melted and subjected to intense conditions so that impurities separate from the pure metal.

Your money and your children are exactly like that process. They are not inherently evil, but they are an extremely difficult test. Navigating them properly requires wisdom, patience and constant awareness of Allah ﷻ. Money can distract you from your purpose. Children can consume all your attention and energy, pulling you away from your spiritual obligations.

The contrast Allah ﷻ presents is striking: وَٱللَّهُ عِندَهُۥٓ أَجْرٌ عَظِيمٌ (and Allah has with Him a great reward). Whatever temporary pleasure or benefit you derive from wealth and children, Allah ﷻ has something far greater waiting for you. This is meant to help you maintain perspective. When you are tempted to compromise your principles for financial gain, remember that Allah's reward is greater. When your children demand so much that you neglect your relationship with your Creator, remember that the ultimate compensation lies with Him.

translation: So fear Allah as much as you are able and listen and obey and spend [in the way of Allah]; it is better for your selves. And whoever is protected from the stinginess of his soul - it is those who will be the successful
commentary:
فَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ مَا ٱسْتَطَعْتُمْ وَٱسْمَعُوا۟ وَأَطِيعُوا۟ وَأَنفِقُوا۟ خَيْرًا لِّأَنفُسِكُمْ ۗ وَمَن يُوقَ شُحَّ نَفْسِهِۦ فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْمُفْلِحُونَ

*So have Taqwa of Allah as much as you are able, and listen and obey and spend; it is better for your own selves. And whoever is protected from the stinginess of his soul, it is those who will be successful.*

The phrase مَا ٱسْتَطَعْتُمْ (as much as you are able) is significant. Allah ﷻ does not say "have Taqwa of Me as much as I deserve." He knows that is beyond human capacity. Instead, He asks for what you can manage. At the end of each day, you should ask yourself: Did I show enough Taqwa? Was I capable of more? Could I have been more conscious of Allah ﷻ in my actions?

Notice that Taqwa here comes in the context of the previous instructions about forgiveness and forbearance with family. So one manifestation of your Taqwa is how forgiving, courteous and overlooking you are with your spouse. When you see your husband or wife in front of you, you should see Allah's expectations. That is a profound shift in perspective. You are not primarily answerable to your spouse for how you treat them; you are answerable to Allah ﷻ. Similarly, their behavior toward you is being evaluated by Allah ﷻ, not by you.

This should fundamentally change how you respond to being wronged. When your spouse disobeys or hurts you, you should not wish for them to be in trouble with Allah ﷻ. Only someone with a truly petty heart would think that way. Instead, your response should be: "O Allah, I know what she did was wrong, but I forgive her. Please forgive her too. I don't want to see her suffering on Judgment Day. I love her too much for that." This is what it means to cover for someone, to make du'a for them even when they have wronged you. That takes a large heart, and it is the essence of real love.

The commands وَٱسْمَعُوا۟ وَأَطِيعُوا۟ (listen and obey) are reminders that we need repeated exposure to these teachings. This is why regular recitation of the Quran is so vital. These instructions need to hit you again and again until they become part of your character, until they come to life in your daily interactions.

Then comes the command: وَأَنفِقُوا۟ خَيْرًا لِّأَنفُسِكُمْ (spend; it is better for your own selves). The spending mentioned here is not random; it connects directly to the family dynamics discussed earlier. When a husband and wife work together as believers, they encourage each other to do more for Allah ﷻ, not less. Spending becomes easier, not harder. The wife does not grab the husband's hand and say, "No, that money was for our new car" or "Why are you giving to the masjid? We were saving for vacation." Instead, they work as a team, motivated by shared values and a shared vision of earning Allah's pleasure.

The ayah then presents one of the most important psychological insights in the Quran: وَمَن يُوقَ شُحَّ نَفْسِهِۦ فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْمُفْلِحُونَ (whoever is protected from the stinginess of his soul, it is those who will be successful). The word شُحَّ  refers to an obsessive greed and self-absorption. It is when you are constantly focused on yourself: what you deserve, what you got, what bothers you, how you feel.

Allah ﷻ is teaching us that one of the primary destroyers of marriage is this self-absorption. When both partners are fixated on their own needs and feelings, collision is inevitable. But whoever can be protected from this self-centered mindset, whoever can learn to prioritize the other person and the relationship over their own ego, these are the ones who achieve true success. This is not just about marriage; it is about ultimate success in this life and the next.

translation: If you loan Allah a goodly loan, He will multiply it for you and forgive you. And Allah is Most Appreciative and Forbearing
commentary:
إِن تُقْرِضُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ قَرْضًا حَسَنًا يُضَـٰعِفْهُ لَكُمْ وَيَغْفِرْ لَكُمْ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ شَكُورٌ حَلِيمٌ

*If you lend to Allah a beautiful loan, He will multiply it for you and forgive you. And Allah is Appreciative and Forbearing.*

Just as in the previous surah, the remedy for stinginess and self-absorption is spending for the sake of Allah ﷻ. The metaphor of قَرْضًا حَسَنًا (a beautiful loan) is remarkable. When you lend to someone, you expect to get your money back. Allah ﷻ is saying: lend to Me, and not only will you get it back, but I will multiply it for you.

This loan must be حَسَنًا (beautiful), meaning it should be given with sincerity, without resentment, without seeking recognition from people. When a couple works together with this mindset, spending becomes a joy rather than a source of conflict. They are not fighting over who gets what; they are competing to see who can give more to Allah ﷻ.

The description of Allah ﷻ at the end of this ayah is profound: شَكُورٌ حَلِيمٌ (Appreciative and Forbearing). The word شَكُور  is usually translated as "grateful," but when applied to Allah ﷻ, it means He is deeply appreciative. He truly appreciates the effort you put forth for His sake, even when that effort is small or imperfect.

And حَلِيمٌ  means He is forbearing, patient and understanding. He knows what you are going through. He feels for you. When you struggle in your marriage, when you try to practice these principles of forgiveness and forbearance but fall short, Allah ﷻ understands your struggle. He sees your effort. He appreciates your intention. This should give us hope and motivation to keep trying, even when we fail.

translation: Knower of the unseen and the witnessed, the Exalted in Might, the Wise
commentary:
عَـٰلِمُ ٱلْغَيْبِ وَٱلشَّهَـٰدَةِ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلْحَكِيمُ

*Knower of the unseen and the witnessed, the Exalted in Might, the Wise.*

The surah closes with a powerful reminder of who Allah ﷻ is. He is عَـٰلِمُ ٱلْغَيْبِ وَٱلشَّهَـٰدَةِ (Knower of the unseen and the witnessed). He knows what is hidden in your heart and what is apparent in your actions. You cannot hide your true intentions from Him. When you forgive your spouse outwardly but hold grudges inwardly, He knows. When you struggle internally but put on a brave face, He knows.

He is ٱلْعَزِيزُ (the Exalted in Might), meaning He has ultimate authority. No matter what challenges you face in your family life, Allah ﷻ has power over all of it. And He is ٱلْحَكِيمُ (the Wise). Everything He has legislated about family relationships comes from perfect wisdom. Even when His commands are difficult, even when practicing forgiveness and forbearance feels impossible, trust that He knows what is best for you.

These final attributes remind us that we are not navigating family life alone. The One who commanded us to be forgiving is watching, appreciating our efforts and ready to help us succeed. He understands our struggles because He is Haleem, and He has the power to change our circumstances because He is Al-Aziz, and His guidance is perfect because He is Al-Hakeem.
